🗳️ Electoral Trend Graph
How free and fair were past elections — and what does the model predict for the next one? A high score means the winner got a genuine mandate from real voters. A low score means the rules were broken. The chart does not predict who wins — it predicts how fairly the election will be run.
📊 A score of 86% means 0.860 on the model's 0–1 scale. Think of it as a democracy health score: 100% = completely fair election, 0% = completely broken. The higher the score, the more likely the winner earned a real mandate from voters.
FieldShift model prediction — what the model expected Validated actual result — what really happened Current prediction — next election ← older elections    newer elections →  When orange & blue lines are close, the model was accurate.

Accuracy evidence by horizon (5 pp tolerance)
Hit rate is the share of elections where absolute error was 5 percentage points or less. 95% CI uses a Wilson interval.

Visualization
Risk Trajectories
📈
What you are looking at
Each colored line is one country. The higher a line sits on the chart, the greater that country's democratic risk in that year. A rising line means things are getting worse.
🎚️
The colored zones
The background shading divides the chart into risk zones: green (low risk) at the bottom, through yellow and orange, to red (high risk) at the top. Most healthy democracies sit in the green zone.
🔘
The layer tabs
Switch between risk layers using the tabs above the chart. "Composite" shows the overall score. The other tabs isolate specific aspects: fiscal health, constitutional integrity, organizational capture, behavioral signals, or extended signals.
📊
The two smaller charts
Below the main chart: a horizontal bar chart ranks countries by their latest score (longest bar = highest risk), and a donut chart shows how many countries fall into each risk category.
⚖️
What the Composite score means
The Composite is an equally weighted average of all five layers — Fiscal, Constitutional, Organizational, Behavioral, and Extended. No single layer dominates: a country that scores poorly on just one dimension will show moderate overall risk, while a country that scores poorly across several will show high composite risk. This matters because democratic erosion rarely happens in isolation — it tends to spread across institutions simultaneously. The Composite captures that systemic pattern in a single number, making it possible to rank countries and track change over time without losing sight of the full institutional picture. Think of it as a temperature reading: one fever is a symptom, but a sustained fever across five vital signs is a diagnosis.
◯

Select countries and run analysis

Choose countries above, then press Run Analysis.

Rankings
Country Scores
Latest available year in selected range. Dash cells indicate genuine data gaps — never imputed.
🏆
How to read the table
Countries are ranked by their composite risk score. The colored bar next to each score shows visually how high the risk is. The badge shows the risk category: Minimal, Low, Medium, High, or Critical.
↗️
Trajectory column
"Trajectory" shows the direction of change over your selected time period. "Deteriorating" means things have been getting worse year after year. "Improving" means the opposite. "Volatile" means large swings in both directions.
—
What the dashes mean
A dash (—) in a cell means the data genuinely does not exist for that country and layer — not that it was zero or unknown. This system never fills in missing data with estimates.
🗂️
The five layer columns
Fiscal = public money, Constitutional = fundamental rules, Organizational = institutional neutrality, Behavioral = leader conduct, Extended = press and civil society freedom. Higher % = more risk in that area.
🌍
Risky Regions
When multiple countries in the same region deteriorate simultaneously, democratic norms compress across the whole neighborhood — making each country's backsliding harder to reverse. This panel shows which regions face the highest current risk and the fastest-widening FSI gap, so you can see where a FieldShift is becoming a regional phenomenon.
□

No data yet

Run an analysis to populate this table.

🌍 Risky Regions — Top 10 Ranking
3+ countries · composite average · 2010→2023 trajectory
What is a risky region? A geographic cluster of countries where democratic erosion is occurring simultaneously — compressing regional norms and making each country's reversal harder. When neighbors backslide together, the political cost of staying democratic rises. Ranked by combined current risk (45%) · rate of deterioration (35%) · breadth across countries (20%).
FieldShift Index
The Mandate–Capacity Gap
Winning an election is not the same as being able to govern.
The FSI measures exactly that gap.
The FieldShift Index measures the distance between the power voters give a government and the power that government can actually use. It does not measure how democratic or authoritarian a country is. It measures a structural condition — the degree to which elections still function as the mechanism connecting public will to public policy.
Component 1
Mandate Strength
How clearly and broadly voters authorized a government to act. Strong mandates come from decisive elections, broad representation, and public legitimacy. Weak mandates reflect fragmented results or declining turnout.
Component 2
Governing Capacity
How much of that authorization can actually be converted into policy. Constrained by courts that block legislation, fiscal rules that bind future budgets, constitutional supermajority requirements, and administrative systems that resist direction.
The Formula
FSI = Mandate − Capacity
▲ Positive — FieldShift condition
● Near zero — Alignment
▼ Negative — Authoritarian or technocratic lock-in
Positive — FieldShift Zone
The government holds a clear mandate but cannot fully act on it. The institutional architecture was not built to respond to the mandate that now exists.
Sweden · USA · Italy · France · Greece
Near Zero — Alignment Zone
Mandate and capacity are aligned. What voters authorize, governments can largely deliver. The reference condition for functioning representative democracy.
South Korea · Japan · Norway · New Zealand
Negative — Lock-in Zone
Governing capacity exceeds democratic mandate. Either authoritarian systems governing without meaningful electoral authorization, or technocratic systems insulating governance from democratic pressure.
Russia · China · Belarus · Singapore
◈

Data Transparency
Unavailable Countries
The following countries cannot be included. No country is silently omitted — each absence is documented.
Early Warning System
Red Flag Indicator Panel
A real-time control system modelled on industrial process monitoring. Each country's institutional state is tracked across 28 indicators in 6 subsystems. When any indicator crosses a threshold, a red flag is raised with a scientifically grounded time-to-action window and prescribed response. The earlier the intervention, the lower the recovery cost.
How it works
28 indicators. 6 subsystems. One country at a time.
Select a country from the dropdown below. The panel instantly calculates all 28 indicators from the country's data and displays them like a control room screen. Each indicator shows its current status, value, and what it is measuring.
Five status levels
● NOMINAL — Safe, no action needed
● WATCH — Monitor closely
● WARNING — Plan an intervention
● CRITICAL — Act within months
● FAULT — Act immediately
Each flag shows three layers
GREEN — Plain language: what it means
ORANGE — Why it matters for democracy
BLUE — Formula, thresholds, sources
The blue column is for scholars who need to verify or replicate the methodology.
The core principle: The earlier an indicator is addressed, the lower the cost of correction. A country at WATCH level today can be stabilised with routine policy adjustments. The same country at CRITICAL level may require years of deliberate institutional rebuilding. The time-to-action windows shown in each flag are calibrated to this empirical asymmetry — the average democratic erosion episode takes 4.7 years; the average recovery takes 8.3 years.
Select country to monitor
or run analysis above and panel updates automatically
Select a country to initialise the monitoring panel
Gap Reduction Model
FieldShift Reduction Framework
A precise action framework specifying what countries must do to narrow the mandate-capacity gap, when they must do it, and in what sequence actions become possible or necessary. Distinguishes between Backshift (democratic restoration) and Frontshift (authoritarian consolidation or high-capacity democratic alignment).
What "Backshift" and "Frontshift" mean
Backshift means a country returning to a better democratic state it once had — like Poland recovering from the judicial capture of 2015-2023. It is restoration.   Frontshift means a country moving to a new alignment it has never had before — either a democracy achieving tighter mandate-capacity fit, or an authoritarian system consolidating power beyond its previous levels. Both are measured and mapped in this framework.
🌳
Decision Tree
A step-by-step guide that tells you which problem to fix first. Like a doctor's diagnosis checklist — you go through each question in order, and the answers determine what action is needed.
🗺️
Country Pathways
12 specific countries with their individual roadmaps. Shows what each country needs to do and in which phase — based on their current position on the FieldShift scale.
📋
Action Matrix
A table of targeted interventions for each of the six ways democracy erodes. Sorted into immediate actions (this year), medium-term actions (1-5 years), and long-term actions (5+ years).
🚦
Alert Thresholds
The four alarm levels — like a traffic light system for democracy — and what is required at each level. Also shows the four monitoring systems that must run continuously.
📝
Action Templates
Ready-made action plans, like checklists, for two types of countries: democracies trying to recover, and authoritarian systems managing their current equilibrium.

Global Map
Democratic Risk — World View
Every country color-coded by composite risk score for the selected year. Hover to inspect. Click to load into Country Story.
🟢
Green countries
Low democratic risk — institutions are functioning well, the mandate-capacity gap is small, and the trajectory is stable or improving.
🟡
Yellow countries
Watch zone — some indicators are showing strain. Not yet critical, but the situation requires monitoring and possibly preventive action.
🟠
Orange countries
Warning zone — multiple indicators are elevated. Deliberate institutional repair is needed within 1-3 years to prevent further erosion.
🔴
Red countries
Crisis zone — democratic erosion is advanced. International attention and sustained institutional rebuilding are required. Recovery will take years.
⚡
Most volatile countries
Volatility means a country's democratic health changes rapidly — either improving or deteriorating faster than its neighbors. High volatility is a warning signal: fast deterioration is harder to catch and reverse than gradual erosion. The Top 10 list to the right of the map shows the countries with the largest and fastest swings across all five institutional layers since 2010. Hover over any country on the list for a full explanation of why it is volatile and what to watch.
How to read this map: Each country is colored by its FieldShift risk score. Green = healthy democracy · Yellow = early warning signs · Orange = active erosion · Red = critical or crisis. Click any country to open its full story. Use the controls below to change year or risk layer.

⚡ Top 10 Most Volatile
Countries with the largest and fastest institutional swings since 2010. Hover for explanation.
How to read the risk score — 0% = perfect democratic health  ·  100% = complete institutional failure
0–25%
Very Low
Stable democracy. All systems functioning.
25–38%
Low
Minor strain. No structural risk.
38–52%
⚠ Watch
Early signals. Monitor closely.
52–66%
⚠ Warning
Significant erosion. Intervention window open.
66–80%
🔴 Critical
Advanced erosion. Reversal is hard.
80–100%
🔴 Crisis
Severe erosion. Democratic failure likely.
Critical threshold (52%): Above this, the FieldShift model identifies active institutional stress — the point where erosion becomes self-reinforcing and reversal requires deliberate political action. Below 38%: democracy is stable. Between 38–52%: a FieldShift is possible but not yet likely. Above 52%: a shift is underway.
Global Rankings
How to read: Countries score 0–100% across five institutional layers. Lower = healthier. Below 25% = stable democracy  ·  38–52% = early warning  ·  above 52% = active erosion (the FieldShift threshold). Tabs show overall or layer-specific rankings. Each card shows what a country does well, what drags its score, and what would need to change to move up. Click any card to open the full Country Story.
Fiscal — Budget transparency & anti-corruption. Low % = sound public finance.
Constitutional — Rule of law & judicial independence. Low % = strong legal order.
Organizational — Electoral integrity & civil service neutrality. Low % = professional institutions.
Behavioral — Elite norm compliance & leader conduct. Low % = norms respected.
Extended — Press freedom & civil society space. Low % = open information environment.

Country Story · pp = percentage points (?)
Narrative Intelligence
Select any country to see its full democratic story — a structured narrative of how its FieldShift profile evolved, which mechanisms drove the change, and what the data means for its future.
📖
For journalists
The Country Story gives you the factual spine of a political story: what changed, in what order, and by how much. Every claim is traceable to the underlying data.
🎓
For researchers
The arc cards show how each risk phase relates to real political events. The layer snapshot at the bottom shows the current profile for quick comparison with other cases.
📚
For students
Each country story shows how abstract concepts — institutional erosion, democratic backsliding, mandate gaps — play out in a real case. Use it as a structured entry point before going deeper into primary sources.
🏛️
For policymakers
The story diagnoses which mechanism is driving risk in this specific country — essential for designing targeted rather than generic interventions.
🗳️
For election and democracy observers
The Country Story gives election monitors and democracy observers a structured pre-mission briefing: which institutional layers were under stress before the election, whether the trajectory was already deteriorating, and how the FSI gap signals whether the incoming government will be able to govern. The five-layer snapshot tells you where to look — a high Organizational score flags electoral administration risk; a high Behavioral score flags candidate or ruling-party norm violations; a high Extended score flags pressure on the press and civil society that can constrain free campaigning. Each card links to documented events with primary-source citations, giving every observation an institutional context. Use it alongside OSCE/ODIHR, Carter Center, or NDI mission reports for a data-grounded baseline before and after polling day.
Select a country

Comparison Mode
Head-to-Head Analysis
Compare any two countries across all dimensions of democratic health. It includes a Democratic Peer Finder showing countries at a similar FSI level and what their trajectories suggest about likely outcomes.
⚖️
Side-by-side layers
Both countries shown with identical scales so you can see at a glance which has stronger fiscal integrity, more constitutional risk, or better behavioral compliance.
🔭
Democratic Peer Finder
Shows countries at a similar FSI level today — and what happened next in comparable cases. A Poland at 0.22 today may resemble Sweden at 0.22 in 2014. What happened next in Sweden?
📰
Auto-generated verdict
A plain-language summary of the key differences between the two countries — which is more at risk, which is improving faster, and where the critical divergences are.

Research Tools — Academic Grade
Advanced Research Features
Three tools designed for democracy researchers and graduate students. Based on the scholarly literature in comparative democratization, with explicit uncertainty quantification, causal mechanism typology (Lührmann & Lindberg 2019), and regional contagion analysis (Brinks & Coppedge 2006).

Concept

        
      

      

    

  







  

    V-Dem publishes 95% confidence intervals for its indicator estimates, derived from its Bayesian IRT measurement model. A country scoring 0.52 ± 0.08 straddles the Watch Zone/Warning threshold — meaning the risk classification itself is uncertain. The shaded bands below show estimated uncertainty ranges around each country's composite score trajectory. Countries within the gray band may be mis-classified by up to one risk tier.
  

  

    

      
Country A — Uncertainty Profile

      
      

      

    

    

      
Country B — Compare Uncertainty

      
      

      

    

  

  

    Methodological note: Uncertainty bands are estimated from cross-source variance (inter-source disagreement) and from V-Dem's published confidence intervals where available. Countries with fewer available sources or high inter-source disagreement show wider bands. The 0.52 dashed line marks the FieldShift early-warning threshold.
  







  

    Based on Lührmann & Lindberg (2019) "A third wave of autocratization is here: What is new about it?" — Democratization 26(7). Three empirically distinct erosion pathways differ in which institutional layer fails first, the speed of deterioration, and the actors driving it. Select a pathway to see its layer-sequence signature, historical cases, and how to detect it in FieldShift data.
  

  

    
    
    
  

  

    Quick pathway reference: each tab corresponds to an erosion mode where the first failing institutional layer, the deterioration pace, and the main actors are distinct. Picking a tab updates the detailed view below and highlights the most relevant FieldShift early-warning signals.
  

  

    

      
        

          Pathway
          What it is
          First deteriorating layer
          Key actors
          FieldShift signal
        

      
      
        

          Executive Aggrandizement
          Legal, incremental power consolidation by elected incumbents
          Behavioral (norm violations)
          Executive, judiciary, media regulators
          Behavioral leads + high FSI gap
        

        

          Executive Takeover
          Rapid seizure of power, often military-backed
          Extended (military/security)
          Military/elite networks, executive
          Extended layer rising + rapid composite jump
        

        

          Populist Norm-Erosion
          Polarized tactics to weaken constraints through elections
          Behavioral (polarization, norms)
          Populist leader, mass mobilization, media influence
          Behavioral leads extended + long gestation
        

      
    

  


  
  

    

      
⚙️

      

        
Executive Aggrandizement

        
Lührmann & Lindberg (2019) · Most common contemporary pathway · 60% of cases 2000–2019

        

          Quick links: 
          Actors ·
          Speed of deterioration
        

      

    

    

      Causal mechanism: An elected incumbent gradually accumulates power through formally legal means — packed courts, reformed electoral laws, captured media regulators, weakened audit institutions — without ever triggering a coup or emergency declaration. Each individual step is defensible; the cumulative effect is autocratization. Bermeo (2016) calls this "executive aggrandizement." The key diagnostic: the executive remains popular while institutions erode.
    

    
Layer deterioration sequence (empirical median across 18 cases):

    

      
LayerOrderEmpirical description

      
        
Behavioral1stNorm violations appear 2–4 years before structural change

        
Organizational2ndMedia capture, civil society restrictions follow

        
Constitutional3rdCourts, electoral bodies formalized under executive control

        
Fiscal4thAudit independence, budget opacity worsen later

        
Extended5thRegional diffusion effects, corruption last

      
    

    
FieldShift early-warning signal:

    

      
SignalLikelihood

      
        
Behavioral leads88%

        
High FSI Gap72%

        
Slow trajectory85%

        
Popular incumbent79%

Bolivia50%

        
Nicaragua82%

      
    

    

  


  
  

    

      
⚔️

      

        
Executive Takeover

        
Lührmann & Lindberg (2019) · Declining in frequency · ~15% of cases 2000–2019

        

          Quick links: 
          Actors ·
          Speed of deterioration
        

      

    

    

      Causal mechanism: A rapid, often military-backed seizure of power that eliminates democratic competition within a short window (typically weeks to months). Unlike aggrandizement, this pathway involves visible rupture: suspension of the constitution, dissolution of parliament, declaration of emergency, or military coup. The extended layer (military expenditure, security sector) typically shows pre-coup elevation. FieldShift composite scores can move 0.20–0.35 points within a single year during takeover episodes.
    

    
Layer deterioration sequence (empirical median across 8 cases):

    

      
LayerOrderEmpirical description

      
        
ExtendedPre-signalMilitary expenditure elevated 1–2 years prior

        
Constitutional1stFormal rupture: constitution suspended or dissolved

        
Organizational1stMedia and parties simultaneously suppressed

        
Behavioral2ndPost-coup normalization of authoritarian behavior

        
Fiscal2ndFiscal institutions captured post-coup

      
    

    
FieldShift early-warning signal:

    

      
SignalLikelihood

      
        
Extended layer rising76%

        
Rapid composite jump91%

        
Low FSI Gap initially63%

      
    

    
Historical cases — click to open Country Story:

    

      
CountryScore

      
        
Thailand69%

        
Myanmar86%

        
Egypt74%

        
Sudan84%

        
Guinea74%

        
Mali80%

      
    

    

  


  
  

    

      
🌊

      

        
Populist Norm-Erosion

        
Svolik (2019) · Growing pathway · Most ambiguous to detect

        

          Quick links: 
          Actors ·
          Speed of deterioration
        

      

    

    

      Causal mechanism: Polarization-driven erosion where a populist leader exploits democratic procedures — elections, referenda, majority rule — to weaken constraints on executive power, while opposition supporters and institutions are delegitimized as corrupt elites or foreign agents. Unlike aggrandizement, this pathway often involves genuine popular support and proceeds through legitimate-seeming democratic channels. Svolik (2019) argues this is the most dangerous contemporary pattern because it is hardest to defend against democratically.
    

    
Layer deterioration sequence (empirical median across 12 cases):

    

      
LayerOrderEmpirical description

      
        
Behavioral1stNorm violations and polarizing rhetoric years before structural change

        
Extended2ndSocial trust declines; corruption perception worsens

        
Organizational3rdOpposition and media delegitimized but not legally suppressed

        
ConstitutionalSlow/StalledFormal institutions resist longer; change incomplete

        
FiscalSlow/StalledFiscal institutions often protected by opposition-controlled legislatures

      
    

    
FieldShift early-warning signal:

    

      
SignalLikelihood

      
        
Behavioral leads extended82%

        
Constitutional holds71%

        
Long gestation (3–8 yr)88%

        
High polarization signal79%

Slovenia26%

      
    

    

  







  

    Brinks & Coppedge (2006) documented that democratic erosion and recovery show systematic geographic clustering — countries tend to follow their neighbors. Boese et al. (2021) confirmed this in the third wave of autocratization. This module shows regional democratic risk averages, spread dynamics, and which countries are most exposed to neighborhood contagion effects.
  

  

    

      

        
Regional Risk Overview — 2023

        

      

      

        
Select a region to see country-level spread analysis

Kyrgyzstan61%↓

            
Uzbekistan60%↓

          
        

        

          Contagion Risk: 5 of 5 countries in Central Asia are in Warning or above (≥52%). Based on Brinks & Coppedge geographic diffusion research, neighboring countries face elevated risk of erosion through three pathways: shared elite networks, emulation of successful suppression techniques, and reduced external democratic pressure when regional democratic density declines.

Contagion Research Basis

          Brinks & Coppedge (2006) found p<0.001 for geographic clustering of regime type. Boese et al. (2021) showed regional autocratization waves propagate within 3–5 years. Countries in high-risk regions face elevated erosion probability independent of domestic factors.
        

      

    

    

  







  


    

      
Documentation & Transparency

      

    


    

      
📐 Methodology

      
⚛ Field Theory

      
🔬 Empirical Testbed

      
〰 Wave Simulation

      
ℹ About & Citation

      
⚛ SocioField Physics

    


    
    

      
FieldShift computes a Composite Democratic Risk Score (CDRS) by aggregating eight independent international datasets across five institutional risk layers. All scores are normalized to 0–1, where 0 = minimum risk and 1 = maximum risk. The composite is the unweighted mean of the five layer scores. This page documents the aggregation logic, source assignments, threshold calibration, and the lead-time differentiation that makes FieldShift an early warning system rather than a snapshot index.


      

        Evidence status
        Validated
        Replicated
        In Development
        Claims in this dashboard are tagged to separate operational outputs from exploratory research hypotheses.
      


      

        

          
Researchers

          
Use Methodology, Testbed, and cross-dataset table for model assumptions, validation, and replication context.

        

        

          
Students

          
Start with risk bands and layer cards, then move to sources and uncertainty notes for interpretation discipline.

        

        

          
Journalists

          
Use country snapshots plus evidence-status tags to separate measured findings from hypotheses in coverage.

        

        

          
General public

          
Use map and story views first; read the threshold and uncertainty notes before drawing strong conclusions.

        

      


      

        ⚠ Current limitation — confidence intervals: Scores are currently presented as point estimates. V-Dem publishes 95% Bayesian confidence intervals for all its indicator estimates; a country scoring 0.52 ± 0.08 is in a qualitatively different situation from one scoring 0.52 ± 0.02. Confidence interval visualization is the highest-priority item in the Version 2.0 development roadmap. Until then, scores in the Watch Zone (0.38–0.52) should be treated with particular caution.
      


      
      

        
Aggregation Formula

        
CDRS = ( Fiscal + Constitutional + Organizational + Behavioral + Extended ) ÷ 5

        
Equal weighting (0.20 per layer) reflects the theoretical position that no single institutional domain is lexically prior in the erosion process. Sensitivity analysis varying weights between 0.10 and 0.30 yields rank-order correlations (Spearman ρ) above 0.94 relative to the equal-weight baseline — indicating high robustness. Full sensitivity tables are in the Methodology Document.

      


      
      
Five Risk Layers — Sources, Indicators, and Lead Times

      


        

          

            
1

            
Fiscal Integrity

            
20%

          

          

            
⏱ Lead time: 3–5 years

            
Sources: IMF Government Finance Statistics · World Bank WGI (Control of Corruption, Govt Effectiveness)

            
Budget transparency, off-budget expenditure, fiscal autonomy of subnational units, debt management. Shifts in education/security spending ratios serve as the earliest detectable signal of a regime's governing philosophy — typically visible 3–5 years before institutional symptoms appear.

          

        


        

          

            
2

            
Constitutional

            
20%

          

          

            
⏱ Lead time: 2–4 years

            
Sources: V-Dem Liberal Component Index · Comparative Constitutions Project · IDEA Electoral Integrity

            
Judicial independence, separation of powers, executive constraints, rule of law. Constitutional amendment proposals and changes to supermajority requirements provide 2–4 years of advance warning of institutional hardwiring attempts (Ginsburg & Huq 2018; Lührmann & Lindberg 2019).

          

        


        

          

            
3

            
Organizational

            
20%

          

          

            
⏱ Lead time: 1–3 years

            
Sources: V-Dem Electoral Component · Freedom House (secondary) · IDEA Electoral Integrity

            
Civil society space, political party pluralism, freedom of assembly, electoral administration. Gatekeeping breakdown — when mainstream parties fail to coordinate against anti-democratic forces — and organised illiberal mobilisation are key 1–3 year precursors (Levitsky & Ziblatt 2018).

          

        


        

          

            
4

            
Behavioral Norms

            
20%

          

          

            
⏱ Lead time: 0–1 years

            
Sources: V-Dem Participatory & Egalitarian Components · RSF Press Freedom Index

            
Elite norm compliance, media freedom, political tolerance, minority inclusion, informal rule-bending by executives. This layer captures the distinction Levitsky & Ziblatt draw between formal constitutional rules and the informal mutual toleration norms that sustain them. Deterioration here is typically the last visible signal before FieldShift equilibrium is established.

          

        


        

          

            
5

            
Extended Resilience

            
20%

          

          

            
⏱ Lead time: varies

            
Sources: SIPRI Military Expenditure DB · V-Dem Deliberative Component · WGI Voice & Accountability

            
Civil–military relations, civilian budget control over security sector, deliberative quality of legislatures, protest capacity. The SIPRI military expenditure ratio — military as % of total government spending — operationalizes civilian budget oversight. Rapidly rising ratios may precede either security-pathway erosion (Myanmar 2021) or information-autocracy onset.

          

        


      


      
      
Risk Band Thresholds — Empirical Calibration

      

        
          

            BandCDRS RangeCountries (2023)ShareCalibration basis
          

        
        
          

            Very Low
            0.00 – 0.241811.0%
            Norway (0.11), Switzerland (0.12)
          

          

            Low
            0.25 – 0.372616.0%
            Germany, France, Japan
          

          

            Watch
            0.38 – 0.513823.3%
            Mexico, South Korea, Brazil
          

          

            Warning
            0.52 – 0.654125.2%
            0.52 threshold: 14 of 17 countries V-Dem reclassified from Electoral Democracy to Electoral Autocracy (2010–2023) had CDRS >0.52 in the preceding year
          

          

            Critical
            0.66 – 0.792616.0%
            Hungary (0.756), Turkey (0.75), Iran
          

          

            Crisis
            0.80 – 1.00148.6%
            Russia (0.92), China (0.89)
          

        
      

      

        Dataset mean (2023): 0.513  ·  Median: 0.518  ·  SD: 0.206  ·  n = 163 countries
      


      
      
Primary Data Sources

      

        
V-Dem v14 Varieties of Democracy Institute, Gothenburg. Bayesian IRT model aggregating expert codings into latent estimates with 95% CI. Gold standard for multidimensional democratic assessment.

        
Freedom House Freedom in the World (2010–2023). Political rights and civil liberties indicators are used as secondary cross-validation where available, primarily in Organizational indicators and selected constitutional/mandate checks.

        
IMF GFS Government Finance Statistics. Central government revenue transparency, off-budget expenditure, and fiscal rules compliance. Primary source for Fiscal layer.

        
World Bank WGI Worldwide Governance Indicators. Unobserved Components Model aggregating 30+ sources. Contributes Control of Corruption, Govt Effectiveness, Voice & Accountability.

        
RSF Reporters Without Borders Press Freedom Index. Structured expert questionnaire across five dimensions. Primary source for media freedom in Behavioral layer.

        
SIPRI Military Expenditure Database. Military as % of total government spending — civilian budget oversight indicator for Extended Resilience layer.

        
CCP Comparative Constitutions Project. Amendment frequency and scope as indicators of institutional stability in Constitutional layer.

        
IDEA Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance. Electoral Integrity sub-component for Organizational layer.

      


      
Cross-Dataset Validation Snapshot

      

        
          

            Datasetℋ (asymmetry, empirical)Early-warning levelPeriodScopeStatus
          

        
        
          

            V-Dem (Liberal Democracy) v161.55–1.96S₁ = √3/81790–2025Broad institutional democracy
            Validated
          

          

            Freedom House (FIW)0.9700.1211973–2026Political rights + civil liberties
            Replicated
          

          

            Polity V1.3630.1701946–2018Executive constraints + competition
            Replicated
          

        
      

      

        Interpretation note: cross-dataset variation is expected because datasets measure different democracy dimensions. Public dashboard outputs are driven by the FieldShift CDRS model; cross-dataset asymmetry comparisons are presented for research transparency and external replication.
      


      
      
Full Methodology Documentation

      
Complete documentation covering aggregation logic, source conflict resolution, sensitivity analysis, measurement invariance, and the development roadmap is available in the two documents below.

      

        📐Methodology Document v1.0
        📄SSRN Working Paper
      

    


    
    


      

        
Theoretical Foundation

        
FieldShift as Institutional Field Theory

        
The FieldShift Early Warning System is not merely a composite index. It is the empirical surface of a deeper mathematical structure: a gauge field theory of institutional change, formulated in analogy with classical electromagnetism and extended to political spacetime.

      


      
Political Spacetime — The Four-Dimensional Field

      

        

          
The Institutional Field 𝓘<sup>μ</sup>

          
FieldShift models institutional risk as a vector field 𝓘μ(x) in a four-dimensional political spacetime:

          
    
            
  • x⁰ = t — historical time (causal, timelike)
    • 
            
  • x¹ = ξ — ideological dimension: liberal democracy → authoritarian
    • 
            
  • x² = ε — economic dimension (Layers 1 + 5)
    • 
            
  • x³ = κ — cultural-organizational dimension (Layers 2 + 3 + 4)
    • 
          

          
The five FieldShift layers are projections of a single underlying field vector onto different institutional axes — which is why their empirical correlations are high (ρ = 0.987–0.998). High correlation is predicted by the theory, not a measurement flaw.

        

        

          
The Conformal Metric

          
The spacetime metric takes the form:

          

            ds² = Ω²(ξ) [−dt² + dξ² + dε² + dκ²]
          

          
The conformal factor Ω(ξ) scales with ideological polarization. When polarization increases, Ω grows — compressing effective time and accelerating erosion dynamics. Testable prediction: polarized democracies erode faster than less polarized ones facing identical pressures.

        

      


      
Gauge Invariance — The Invariant Observable

      

        
Why Rhetoric Cannot Be Measured — But the Gap Can

        
The gauge transformation 𝓘μ → 𝓘μ + ∂μφ represents rhetorical or symbolic actions that redefine perceived institutional capacity without changing material content. The gauge-invariant observable is the field strength tensor:

        

          ℱμν = ∂μ𝓘ν − ∂ν𝓘μ
        

        
ℱ0i components capture the gap between formal institutional capacity and informal practice — the mandate-capacity gap at the core of the FieldShift framework. This gap is observable even when rhetorical signals obscure the underlying institutional reality.

      


      
The Field Equations

      

        

          
Maxwell Analogue — Propagation

          

            ∂ν ℱμν = Jμ
          

          
Jμ is the institutional source current — grievances, power concentration, economic stress, legitimacy loss. These sources generate the institutional risk field that the five CDRS layers measure.

        

        

          
Phase Transition — Landau–Ginzburg

          

            F[ψ] = ∫d³x [a|ψ|² + b|ψ|⁴ + c|∇ψ|²]
          

          
When parameter a crosses zero — corresponding to CDRS crossing 0.52 — the system undergoes spontaneous symmetry breaking: the democratic equilibrium becomes unstable and the system falls to an autocratic ground state.

        

      


      
The Coupling Constant g — Institutional Sensitivity

      

        
What Determines How Strongly Grievances Couple to Institutional Pressure

        

          

            

              g = g₀ · (D · H) / L
            

            
D = institutional reversibility (Constitutional layer)
H = democratic stock — accumulated years under democratic rule
L = legitimacy threshold
g₀ = baseline coupling, calibrated historically

          

          

            
Low g: Strong design, long history. Grievances absorbed without producing system-level pressure. Switzerland, Norway.

            
High g: Weak reversibility, short democratic history. Same external shock produces dramatically stronger institutional response.

          

        

      


      
Universal Justice — Why Despots Fall

      

        
WKB Tunneling and the Instability of Autocratic Equilibria

        
Autocratic systems are metastable states — local energy minima with higher free energy than democratic equilibria. They decay through tunneling at rate:

        

          Γ ~ exp(−2 ∫ |p(x)| dx)
        

        
Weaker institutions, stronger civil society, and greater accumulated grievances all reduce the barrier and accelerate collapse. This is the precise mathematical formulation of the intuition that sooner or later the despots fall — not a moral claim but a topological one.

      


      
Publication Roadmap

      

        

          
An appropriate journal (2026)

          
Part I + II: Theory, mechanisms, and 163-country empirical evidence. Establishes FieldShift as a named concept in democratic theory.

        

        

          
A technical journal (under preparation)

          
Full field theory: gauge invariance, conformal metric, Landau–Ginzburg phase transitions, WKB tunneling. Universal applicability to sociology, economics, and organizational theory.

        

      


    


    
    


      

        
Research Infrastructure

        
This Dashboard as Empirical Testbed

        
The FieldShift dashboard serves both as an early-warning interface and as an empirical testbed for institutional-change theory — 728 country-year observations across 163 countries, structured to test four core predictions derived from the model.

      


      
The Five Layers as Field Components

      

        

          
Reframing the High Correlations

          
Inter-layer correlations range 0.987–0.998 (2023, n=163). Under standard composite index logic: problematic redundancy. Under field theory: confirmation that five layers measure projections of one underlying field.

          
The question shifts from "are layers independent?" to "does the field strength tensor ℱμν — computed from layer differences over time — add predictive power beyond CDRS?" This is Test T1.

        

        

          
The Testbed Dataset

          

            

              
728

              
country-year obs.

            

            

              
163

              
countries scored

            

            

              
14

              
years (2010–2023)

            

            

              
5

              
field components

            

          

        

      


      
Four Empirical Tests of the Field Theory

      


        

          

            
T1

            

              
Field Strength Tensor as Predictor

              
Tests gauge invariance — the core claim of the field theory

            

            
IN DEVELOPMENT

          

          

            
Hypothesis: ℱμν — layer-pair differences over time — predicts V-Dem regime reclassifications better than CDRS composite (current AUC = 0.669).

            
Data needed: fieldshift_cdrs_2010_2023.csv + V-Dem v15. Available in this repository.

          

        


        

          

            
T2

            

              
Conformal Factor — Polarization Accelerates Erosion

              
Tests the metric structure of political spacetime

            

            
IN DEVELOPMENT

          

          

            
Hypothesis: Controlling for CDRS level, countries with higher ideological polarization show steeper CDRS trajectories per year — Ω(ξ) compresses effective institutional time.

            
Data needed: FieldShift 18-country panel + V-Dem v2x_polarization.

          

        


        

          

            
T3

            

              
Coupling Constant g — Institutional Sensitivity

              
Tests whether g = g₀·(D·H)/L predicts shock response

            

            
IN DEVELOPMENT

          

          

            
Hypothesis: Countries with high g show larger CDRS responses to identical external shocks than low-g countries.

            
Data needed: FieldShift layers + World Bank GDP + V-Dem democratic stock.

          

        


        

          

            
T4

            

              
WKB Tunneling — Autocratic Survival Times

              
Tests the universality claim — why despots fall

            

            
IN DEVELOPMENT

          

          

            
Hypothesis: Autocratic survival time follows Γ ~ exp(−2∫|p(x)|dx), computed from FieldShift layer scores as proxies for barrier height and width.

            
Data needed: V-Dem episode data + FieldShift Crisis/Critical trajectories (14 countries, 2023).

          

        


      


      
Validation Results to Date

      

        

          
Sensitivity Analysis — Confirmed

          
25 weight configurations. Spearman ρ range: 0.9998–1.000. Country rankings extremely robust — consistent with field theory prediction that layers measure a single underlying field.

        

        

          
Open Science

          
Dataset: fieldshift_cdrs_2010_2023.csv (728 rows). Code: validation.py (959 lines, MIT). Documentation: README.md (CC BY 4.0). All four field theory tests (T1–T4) are designed to run against the published dataset.

        

      


    


    
    


      

        The FieldShift field equations — analogues of Maxwell's equations — admit numerical solution using the
        Finite Difference Time Domain (FDTD) method. This enables direct simulation of how
        institutional pressure waves propagate through the three-dimensional political field space
        (ξ = ideology, ε = economy, κ = culture). The results below show computed wave dynamics for
        an economic shock at ε = 0.25, propagating across the (ε, κ) plane at three polarization levels.
        This is a live JavaScript simulation — adjust the parameters to explore the theory.
      


      

        🌊 Key prediction: An economic shock propagates to the cultural dimension with delay
        Δt = d·Ω(ξ)/c₀ (1D) or Δt ≈ d·Ω(ξ)/(2c₀) (3D, accounting for spherical spreading).
        Highly polarised countries (high Ω) see slower cross-dimensional spread of economic
        shocks — but faster within-dimension erosion due to time compression. This creates a
        counterintuitive pattern: polarised polities erode faster internally but are more
        isolated from cross-dimensional contagion. Directly falsifiable against V-Dem lag data.
      


      
      

        

          
IDEOLOGICAL POLARIZATION ξ

          
          0.50
        

        

          
SHOCK AMPLITUDE

          
          1.0
        

        

          
ALPHA (Ω STEEPNESS)

          
          2.0
        

        
        
      


      
      

        
Ω(ξ) = 1.50

        
c_eff = 0.67

        
ε→κ delay = ~2.4 yr

        
Time step = 0

        
Ready

      


      
      

        

          
t = 0 (INITIAL SHOCK)

          
        

        

          
t = T/3 (SPREAD)

          
        

        

          
t = T (ESTABLISHED WAVE)

          
        

      

      

        
ε (economy) →

        
ε (economy) →

        
ε (economy) →

      


      
      

        
Time series: Field amplitude at κ = 0.75 (cultural probe point)

        
        

          Shows how the institutional shock arrives in the cultural dimension. Delay time increases with ξ (polarization).
        

      


      
      

        

          
What the simulation shows

          
A Gaussian shock pulse is initiated in the economic dimension (ε = 0.25) and propagates as a wave in the (ε, κ) plane. The wave front moves at speed c_eff = 1/Ω(ξ). At high polarization (large Ω), the wave front is slower in spatial dimensions but amplitude damping is weaker — energy stays near the source.

          
Note: this is a 2D projection of a 4D problem. The full simulation also includes the ξ dimension and time evolution of Ω(ξ) self-consistently.

        

        

          
Empirical testability

          
The theory predicts the ε→κ delay (economic shock → cultural institutional response) is proportional to Ω(ξ). Calibration against the 2008 crisis gives:

          

            Nordic region (ξ≈0.15): ~5.5 years

            Brazil (ξ≈0.55): ~3.2 years

            Hungary (ξ≈0.85): ~1.8 years
          

          
Formal test T2 (V-Dem polarization data) is planned for Q4 2026.

        

      


      
Numerical Result — FDTD v1.0 (March 2026)

      

        

          
SIMULATION PARAMETERS

          

            Grid: 60×60×60 · Steps: 300 · dt: 0.0064

            Ω(ξ) = 1 + 2ξ² · γ_damp = 0.008 · CFL = 0.38

            Shock: ε = 0.3, σ_ε = 0.06 · Cultural probe: κ = 0.8

            Language: Python 3 · Method: Leapfrog FDTD (Yee 1966)
          

        

        

          
PROPAGATION RESULT

          

            

              
0.155

              
Δt measured (3D)

            

            

              
0.300

              
Δt predicted (1D)

            

            

              
1.94×

              
3D correction factor

            

            

              
1.000

              
c_eff at ξ=0

            

          

        

      


      

        
NEW FINDING — 3D GEOMETRIC CORRECTION

        

          The 1D theoretical prediction Δt = d·Ω(ξ) overestimates the propagation delay by a factor of ~2.
          This is physically expected: in 3D, spherical wave spreading concentrates energy at the wavefront,
          arriving at the probe earlier than the 1D ray approximation predicts. This factor (~2) is derivable
          analytically from the Green's function of the 3D wave equation and constitutes a new
          theoretical prediction specific to the institutional field theory: the 3D correction
          factor depends on the dimensionality of the institutional space — testable by comparing
          cross-dimensional vs within-dimensional shock propagation speeds in historical data.
        

      


      
Effective Speed c_eff by Polarization Level

      

        

          

            Polarization levelξ valueΩ(ξ) = 1+2ξ²
            c_eff = 1/ΩΔt(ε→κ) 3DV-Dem test population
          

          
            
Low (centrist)ξ = 0.01.0001.0000.15 yrNorway, Switzerland

            
Moderateξ = 0.51.5000.6670.23 yrBrazil, South Korea

            
Highξ = 0.82.2800.4390.34 yrHungary, Poland 2020

            
Extremeξ = 1.03.0000.3330.45 yrRussia, Venezuela

          
        

      


      
Technical Note — FDTD Implementation

      

        Full 3D simulation solves ∂²φ/∂t² = (1/Ω²(ξ))·[∂²φ/∂ξ² + ∂²φ/∂ε² + ∂²φ/∂κ²] − γ·∂φ/∂t
        using the leapfrog FDTD scheme (Yee 1966, extended to 3D). Grid: 60×60×60, CFL factor 0.38,
        absorbing boundaries (Dirichlet). The JavaScript simulation above is a 2D projection (N=80)
        for real-time browser rendering. Full Python code: fieldshift_fdtd.py
        (FieldShift FDTD v1.0, March 2026). Archived in SSRN priority supplement.
        Priority claim: first FDTD simulation of an institutional field in political science, with
        empirically testable 3D correction factor.
      


    


    
    


      


      

        
163
Countries scored

        
14
Years (2010–2023)

        
8
Source datasets

        
5
Risk layers

        
0.513
Global mean CDRS 2023

      


      

        

          
What is FieldShift?

          
FieldShift names the durable gap between electoral authorisation and governing capacity that elections alone cannot close. Legalistic institutional change can weaken democratic constraints within a single electoral cycle, but rebuilding legitimacy, administrative capacity, and shared informational foundations requires sustained coordination over time.

          
The FieldShift Early Warning System operationalizes this framework as an interactive monitoring instrument — translating the depth of academic datasets (above all V-Dem) into a form accessible to policy analysts, educators, journalists, and researchers without specialist quantitative training.

        

        

          
Three Core Mechanisms

          
Legitimacy loss: Trust depreciates faster than it can be rebuilt. Institutions that have lost public confidence cannot regain it through electoral turnover alone.

          
Informational fragmentation: Polarised audiences interpret reform efforts as power grabs. No measure commands broad legitimacy when citizens inhabit distinct informational ecosystems.

          
Institutional lock-in: Changes create beneficiaries with stakes in the new order. Path-dependent appointments and resource control entrench prior alignments through mechanisms that persist beyond the electoral cycle that produced them.

        

      


      
What Could FieldShift Be Used For?

      

        
This is a question worth addressing directly — and honestly. FieldShift is still a research framework. The mathematical theory needs empirical testing, the model needs validation against historical cases, and the four planned field theory tests (T1–T4) have not yet been run. What follows are potential applications, not proven ones.


        
If the framework holds up under testing, it could help with:


        


          

            
DEMOCRACY SUPPORT

            
Early detection of democratic erosion — potentially 3–5 years before institutional symptoms become visible. Giving democracy assistance organisations, the EU, and national governments time to act rather than react.

          


          

            
INSTITUTIONAL DESIGN

            
A quantitative basis for designing institutions that are resilient — not just formally independent, but structurally reversible. The coupling constant g = g₀·(D·H)/L identifies which design choices matter most for a given country's context.

          


          

            
ECONOMIC REFORM SEQUENCING

            
Economic reforms fail not because they are economically wrong but because they are institutionally mistimed. The field theory's phase transition model suggests when institutional conditions are stable enough to absorb reform — and when they are not.

          


          

            
PEACE AGREEMENTS & CRISES

            
The gauge-invariant field strength tensor ℱμν measures the gap between formal commitments and institutional reality. Applied to peace agreements or financial stabilization programs, it could distinguish genuine institutional change from rhetorical compliance.

          


        


        

          
The deeper potential — if empirical testing confirms the theory — is that it could give institutions like the UN, IMF, EU, and national governments a shared quantitative language for institutional vulnerability. Today, policymakers discuss "weak institutions" in roughly the way physicians discussed "bad air" before germ theory: they can observe the symptoms, but the causal mechanism is unclear and treatment is imprecise.

          
A validated field theory of institutional change would not solve these problems. But it might give those working on them a more precise vocabulary — one that connects observable indicators to underlying dynamics, and that works across domains: political science, economics, sociology, and organizational theory.

          
These are aspirations, not claims. The framework is in active development. The four empirical tests described in the Testbed tab will determine how much of this holds up.

        

      


      
Theoretical Foundations

      

        The FieldShift framework draws on and extends five theoretical traditions:
        
    
          
  • Autocratic legalism (Scheppele 2018) — how executives concentrate power through formally legal means
    • 
          
  • Gatekeeping theory (Levitsky & Ziblatt 2018) — when mainstream parties successfully isolate anti-democratic forces
    • 
          
  • Constitutional hardwiring (Buchanan/Ginsburg & Huq) — how temporary advantages become permanent constraints
    • 
          
  • Uncivil society (Bünte 2026) — organised illiberal mobilisation as accelerant of legitimacy loss
    • 
          
  • Fiscal signaling (APSA 2025) — education/security spending ratios as 3–5 year leading indicators
    • 
        

        The asymmetry between erosion and recovery is empirically grounded: V-Dem episode analysis shows recovery episodes average 8.3 years versus 4.7 years for erosion (p < 0.001, n=78 episodes). Croissant & Lott (2024, V-Dem WP 149) independently confirm that resilience capacities predict prevention but not recovery — the capacities that defend democracies from shocks are distinct from those that enable rebuilding after erosion.
      


      
Academic Citation

      

        Hedlund Leijon, T. (2026). FieldShift Early Warning System [Interactive empirical testbed]. Karlstad University. Available at: [URL]


        Hedlund Leijon, T. (2026). FieldShift, Part I: Why Democratic Erosion Outpaces Democratic Recovery. Journal of Democracy (under review).


        Hedlund Leijon, T. (2026). FieldShift, Part II: Evidence, Implications, and an Early Warning System. Journal of Democracy (under review).


        Hedlund Leijon, T. (2026). FieldShift: A General Theory of Threshold-Driven Political Change. American Political Science Review (in preparation).


        This dashboard is the empirical testbed for the field theory of institutional change. The five CDRS layers are field components of a gauge-invariant institutional vector field 𝓘μ. The four planned empirical tests (T1–T4) will be reported in the APSR paper.
      


      
Related Publications

      

        📄SSRN Working Paper
        📐Methodology Document v1.0
        🏛Journal of Democracy (under review)
        ⚛Field Theory
        🔬Empirical Testbed
      


      
Limitations and Roadmap

      

        

          
Current Limitations

          
No confidence intervals: Scores are point estimates. V-Dem's Bayesian CIs will be integrated in Version 2.0.

          
Equal layer weighting: Theoretically motivated but empirically untested against historical regime transitions.

          
Trajectory coverage: Full 2010–2023 trajectory analysis covers the volatile-country subset; not yet all 163 countries.

          
English-language only: Narratives and pedagogical content require multilingual extension for full global accessibility.

        

        

          
Development Roadmap

          
Version 2.0: Confidence interval visualization using V-Dem's published CI bounds. Dynamic regional contagion module.

          
Version 2.1: Institutional event annotation on time series. Pre-election baseline reports (PDF, rolling calendar).

          
Version 3.0: Sensitivity analysis interface (user-adjustable layer weights). API access for R and Python. Subnational risk module.

        

      


      
Contact & Collaboration

      

        Thomas Hedlund Leijon — Independent researcher, Karlstad University, Sweden.

        Research focus: democratic erosion, institutional resilience, and the relationship between electoral turnover and governing capacity.

        Developing the FieldShift framework through comparative analysis of democratic erosion and recovery episodes across regions.


        Collaboration inquiries, data corrections, and methodological feedback are welcome. For V-Dem Institute inquiries regarding pedagogical use of V-Dem data, contact the V-Dem Institute directly at the University of Gothenburg.
      


    


  











  

  
Asymmetry & Recovery

  
Recovery–Erosion Asymmetry Framework

  
Democratic erosion and recovery are not symmetric processes. This framework quantifies the asymmetry empirically: recovery requires more institutional effort and time than the preceding erosion consumed. Across V-Dem v16 (199 countries, 1790–2025), the recovery-to-erosion magnitude ratio ranges from 1.55 to 1.96 across three episode definitions and two magnitude measures.


  

    
Democratic recovery requires more institutional effort than the preceding erosion. The empirical asymmetry observed in V-Dem v16 (factor 1.55–1.96 across episode definitions) implies that the cost of inaction compounds nonlinearly.

    
Working summary — manuscript in pre-submission review

  


  
  

    

      
Erosion

      
Magnitude & Duration

      
Magnitude (Δ) is how deep the fall — peak score minus current score on the V-Dem Liberal Democracy Index. Duration is how long erosion has been ongoing. Together they produce the erosion rate, which is the core driver of ℋ.

    

    

      
ℋ — Asymmetry Multiplier

      
The Recovery Hardness Factor

      
ℋ = 1 + (erosion rate × 10). A symmetric case (ℋ = 1.0) would mean recovery takes exactly as long as erosion. Every 0.01/yr of erosion rate adds 0.1 to ℋ — so a country eroding at 0.04/yr faces a 40% longer recovery than the base estimate.

    

    

      
Illustrative recovery estimate

      
Recovery ≈ Δ × ℋ_emp × ℋ_country × 10 yrs

      

        ℋ_country = 1 + (Δ/dur × 10)

        ℋ_emp ≈ 1.55–1.96 — empirical asymmetry range (V-Dem v16)

        Indicative only — not a deterministic forecast
      

    

  


  

    
    
    
Election lock window: 24 months

  


  
  

    
Asymmetry calculator — enter V-Dem Liberal Democracy Index values

FieldShift · Global Calendar

    
Upcoming Elections Worldwide


    

      
⚠ Illustrative prototype — not a verified election database

      
The dates below are illustrative and have not been verified against authoritative election commission sources. They are included to demonstrate the lock-workflow concept, not as a reference calendar. The CDRS values shown are accurate (derived from V-Dem v16), but the election dates may be outdated or estimated. For authoritative dates, consult IDEA's Voter Turnout Database or national electoral commissions.

    


    
All national-level elections scheduled within the next 24 months, sorted by date — closest first. Color indicates the FieldShift democratic health assessment based on V-Dem scores.

    
CDRS zones: Democracy Valley > 0.70 · Warning Zone 0.35–0.70 · Risk Zone S₁ (≈0.22)–0.35 · Autocracy Pit < S₁

    
Click a country to start the lock workflow. Write your predicted outcome, then confirm with y/yes to lock or n/no to cancel. Locked predictions persist across page reloads and can be exported as a report.

Step 1 — FieldShift Electoral Math Model Prediction:

Safe zone · ≥ 0.60

      
Below inflection

      
Institutional buffers largely intact. Recovery cost remains proportional. Monitoring recommended — erosion can accelerate faster than resilience builds.

    

    

      
Warning zone · 0.40–0.59

      
Stress present

      
Asymmetry is moderate but rising. Recovery still possible without external support. Delay is costly — intervention window is closing.

    

    

      
Risk zone · 0.20–0.39

      
Nonlinear dynamics

      
Recovery requires ℋ× more effort than erosion. Institutional structures have been actively reshaped. External anchoring (EU, treaty) significantly improves recovery odds.

    

    

      
Critical zone · < 0.20

      
Severe path dependency

      
Recovery historically rare without elite defection or external shock. Generational change, not institutional reform, becomes the primary mechanism. Venezuela, Hungary at risk.

    

  









  

    FieldShift Early Warning System — Research-grade approximations based on published open datasets. Not an official assessment.

    Sources: V-Dem Project · Freedom House · World Bank WGI · IMF GFS · SIPRI · RSF Press Freedom Index · Comparative Constitutions Project

⚠ Module under construction

        
This SocioField Physics module is not yet connected to country-specific V-Dem data and currently displays placeholder values. It is hidden from the main navigation and retained here only for development purposes. The scientific results in the manuscript are derived independently from this module and are unaffected.

      


      

        
Social FieldShift Framework · Korbel · Galam · Borghesi–Bouchaud · Halstenberg

        
SocioField Physics · Live Model Readout

        
All five mathematical models running live on FieldShift country data. New functions: hysteresisWidth ★ · fieldAcceleration ★ · galamBasins ★ · normFieldStrength ★ · recoveryPathwayScore ★ · earlyWarningComposite ★ · hierarchicalCoupling (modified)

RFIM · Regime Alignment & Tipping · Korbel et al. 2025

          
          

          

          

        

        

          
Carrier Groups · Institutional Rigidity β · INIS 2019

          

          

            
Regime Alignment m

            

              
Σwₖ tanh(βₖJ̃m)

              
∫p(θ)tanh[β(J̃m+h)]dθ

L=2 Meso

Recovery Pathway Score ★ · NEW

          

            

              

              

            

            

          

          
R = 0.25(1−tip) + 0.20p* + 0.15(1−W/2) + 0.15r_rec + 0.15D/0.6 + 0.10(m+1)/2

        

      


      
      

        

          
Early Warning Signals ★ · earlyWarningComposite()

          
Active:  · Avg severity: 

        

        

      


      
      

        

          
Core Equations · Modules 1–2

          
H = −J Σᵢⱼ Aᵢⱼ sᵢ sⱼ − Σᵢ hᵢ sᵢ
Korbel et al. 2025 · RFIM Hamiltonian

          
m = p·tanh[β(J̃m+h⁺)] + (1−p)·tanh[β(J̃m−h⁻)]
Self-consistency · β = Institutional Rigidity

          
W = max|m_fwd(h)−m_bwd(h)| · dm/dh spikes near h_c ★
hysteresisWidth() · fieldAcceleration() — NEW

          
m = Σₖ wₖ·tanh(βₖ·J̃·m) · χₖ = βₖ(1−mₖ²)
INIS 2019 · Carrier group mean field

        

        

          
Core Equations · Modules 3–6 ★

          
p_{t+1} = a + (1−a−k)[Σ C(r,j)pʲ(1−p)^{r-j}] + k(1−p)
★ galamBasins(): sweep p₀ → bistability + tipPoint

          
∂φ/∂t = D∇²φ + φ(1−φ²)/τ + η · C(r)=−ln(r/L)/(2π)
★ normFieldStrength(): shock PDE → mean/variance/fragmented

          
dMₗ/dt = −k·Mₗ(Mₗ²−1) + Σ Cₗₗ'·Mₗ'
★ F_l restoring force + cascade + r_rec — MODIFIED

          
R = 0.25(1−tip)+0.20p*+0.15(1−W/2)+0.15r_rec+0.15D/0.6+0.10(m+1)/2
★ recoveryPathwayScore() + earlyWarningComposite() — NEW
